text: we find ourselves on the outskirts because of our love for the creepy, odd, and weird? Or does it work the other way around? Do spooky things naturally call out to those of us who feel like we've never quite belonged anywhere? Does the horror genre give us a safe space to be ourselves, our true selves —a self others don't understand and never will, a self others find spooky and weird? I suspect it's probably a mix of the two. In any case, C. S. Lewis said it best when he said friendship was born “at that moment when one person says to another: "What! You too? I thought I was the only one!"” Isn't that what it's like when we encounter another like-minded macabre? We realize we're not alone in our fascination with the darkness; and even more importantly, we realize there's nothing wrong with that fascination at all. We take comfort in our common, shared ground—ground which is often foggy, damp, covered by darkness, littered with broken tombstones, zombie hands bursting from the ground, grasping fitfully at the uncaring sky. In many ways—in most ways—our fascination with these things lands us in an “in-between” place in society. We're here, but sometimes (lots of times, maybe), we're not sure where we belong, or if we belong at all. A little melodramatic, I suppose, but I know it's true for me, and suspect it's true for lots of other horror or “weird” folks also. Herein lies the purpose behind Liminal Spaces —an anthology offering dark speculative stories about the in-between. Sometimes literal, sometimes supernatural and ghostly, sometimes figurative, metaphorical, and pyschological, but the in-between, regardless. Neither here, nor there. Strange stories about people and places lost in a shadowy, hazy middle-ground. Sometimes people are thrust there against their own will. Other times—perhaps even more terrifying—they become lost in an in-between place of their own making. I hope you enjoyed this first installment of Liminal Spaces as much as I enjoyed editing it. I invited these writers, and simply asked them to interpret what a “liminal space” meant to them. They did a wonderful job. And of course I knew they would. That's why I invited them. As Gus Gutherie from Todd Keisling's “Midnight in the Southland” might say, Fare thee well, fellow traveler. Until we meet again in this shadowy liminal space. Kevin Lucia September 22, 2021 Castle Creek, NY The Writers: RICHARD THOMAS is the award-winning author of eight books— Disintegration and Breaker (Penguin Random House Alibi), Transubstantiate, Herniated Roots, Staring Into the Abyss, Tribulations, Spontaneous Human Combustion (Turner Publishing), and The Soul Standard (Dzanc Books). He has been nominated for the Bram Stoker, Shirley Jackson, Thriller, and Audie awards. Visit www.whatdoesnotkillme.com for more information. JOSHUA PALMATIER is a fantasy author with a PhD in mathematics. He currently writes in his “spare” time, edits anthologies, and runs the anthology-producing small press Zombies Need Brains LLC. His most recent fantasy novel, Reaping the Aurora , concludes the fantasy series begun in Shattering the Ley and Threading the Needle , although you can also find his “Throne of Amenkor” series and the “Well of Sorrows” series still on the shelves. You can find out more at www.joshuapalmatier.com or at the small press’ site www.zombiesneedbrains.com. Or follow him on Twitter as @bentateauthor or @ZNBLLC. KELLI OWEN is an American author, editor, reviewer, podcaster, and indie film producer, who has spoken at the CIA Headquarters in Langley, VA regarding both her writing and the field in general. As a member of both the Horror Writers Association and the International Thriller Writers, she has published over a dozen books, including The Headless Boy, Teeth, and Wilted Lilly (a YA series). Her short fiction has appeared in Bram Stoker Award-nominated anthologies alongside Neil Gaiman, Stephen King, Robert McCammon, F. Paul Wilson, and Josh Malerman, among others. For more information, please visit her website at kelliowen.com. MARK ALLAN GUNNELLS loves to tell stories. He has since he was a kid, penning one-page tales that were Twilight Zone knockoffs. He likes to think he has gotten a little better since then. He loves reader feedback, and above all he loves telling stories. He lives in Greer, SC, with his husband Craig A. Metcalf. JESSICA MCHUGH is a novelist, poet, and internationally-produced playwright running amok in the fields of horror, sci-fi, young adult, and wherever else her peculiar mind leads. She's had twenty-five books published in thirteen years, including her bizarro romp, The Green Kangaroos , her YA series, The Darla Decker Diaries , and her Bram Stoker Award-Nominated blackout poetry collection, A Complex Accident of Life. For more info about publications and blackout poetry commissions, please visit McHughniverse.com. TODD KEISLING is the Bram Stoker Award-nominated author of Devil’s Creek . His other books include Scanlines, The Final Reconciliation, and The Monochrome Trilogy , among many shorter works. He lives somewhere in the wilds of Pennsylvania with his family where he is at work on his next novel. MICHAEL WEHUNT lives in the woods of Atlanta with his partner and dog. His stories have appeared in multiple best-of anthologies and other well-known spooky homes. His debut fiction collection, Greener Pastures , shortlisted for the Crawford Award, a Shirley Jackson Award finalist, and the winner of Spain's Premio Amaltea for Foreign Translation, is available from Apex Publications. Find Michael in the trees or online at www.michaelwehunt.com . ANTHONY J. RAPINO resides in Northeastern Pennsylvania, where he teaches English, writes speculative fiction, sculpts three-dimensional horror, and always leaves a jack-o’-lantern burning on the front porch. His short work has appeared in On Spec, Acapella Zoo, Black Ink Horror, Madhouse, Drabblecast, and more. Anthony’s novel, Soundtrack to the End of the World , and story collection, Greetings from Moon Hill , are both available now. Proof of psychosis and a full bibliography can be found on his website: www.anthonyjrapino.com . GWENDOLYN KISTE is the Bram Stoker Award-winning author of The Rust Maidens, Boneset & Feathers, And Her Smile Will Untether
